bibliographic.citation: Wilhelm Linow, Þe Desputisoun bitwen þe Bodi and þe Soule, Erlanger Beiträge zur englischen Philologie 1 (Erlangen and Leipzig: A. Deichert'sche Verlagsbuchhandlung Nachf. [Georg Böhme], 1889).
bibliographic.title: Þe Desputisoun bitwen þe Bodi and þe Soule

entities.description: A parallel scholarly edition of the Auchinleck, Laud, Vernon, and Digby versions, with introduction, witness descriptions, a separate Simeon apparatus, and textual notes.
entities.label: Wilhelm Linow's 1889 edition of four Middle English body-and-soul debate versions
notes: Linow explicitly rejects constructing a single synthetic critical text because of witness contamination and dialectal differences.
The Auchinleck and Laud versions alternate across printed pp. 24-65; Vernon and Digby alternate across pp. 66-105.
Linow's Middle English study occupies pp. 1-112; the four witness texts encoded in CME00130 occupy pp. 24-105.
Hermann Varnhagen's Old French edition begins on p. 113 and Theodore Martin's later English adaptation begins on p. 197; neither is part of CME00130.

rights.basis: The monograph was published in 1889 and is in the United States public domain by publication date. This assessment is separate from provider terms on the BSB scan.
rights.component: Linow's 1889 text, introduction, apparatus, and notes

entities.description: The intermediate transcript from which Linow says he prepared the Auchinleck text.
entities.label: Hermann Varnhagen's working transcript of the Auchinleck version
notes: Modeled provisionally as manuscript_witness because the schema has no dedicated transcript entity type; it is a modern working transcript, not a medieval codex.
Its present location, medium, and survival have not been established.

entities.description: The intermediate transcript from which Linow says he prepared the Laud text.
entities.label: Mr. Parker's working transcript of the Laud Misc. 108 version
notes: Modeled provisionally as manuscript_witness because the schema has no dedicated transcript entity type; it is a modern working transcript, not a medieval codex.
Its present location, medium, and survival have not been established.

entities.description: The intermediate transcript from which Linow says he prepared the Vernon text.
entities.label: Mr. Parker's working transcript of the Vernon version
notes: Modeled provisionally as manuscript_witness because the schema has no dedicated transcript entity type; it is a modern working transcript, not a medieval codex.
Its present location, medium, and survival have not been established.

entities.description: The intermediate transcript from which Linow says he prepared the Digby text.
entities.label: Hermann Varnhagen's working transcript of the Digby 102 version
notes: Modeled provisionally as manuscript_witness because the schema has no dedicated transcript entity type; it is a modern working transcript, not a medieval codex.
Its present location, medium, and survival have not been established.

access.accessMethod: Open the NLS Auchinleck Project headnote or complete manuscript transcription.
access.entityLabel: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.2.1 (Auchinleck Manuscript)

entities.description: A major Middle English literary codex produced in London in the 1330s; Scribe 1 copied the body-and-soul debate at ff. 31vb-35ra, ending on a mutilated stub.

holding.notes: The poem occupies ff. 31vb-35ra; the final folio survives as a damaged stub.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.2.1 (Auchinleck Manuscript)
notes: The NLS headnote counts 591 lines after excluding Latin speech labels and the English title; DIMEV and the nonempty XML L count yield 592. The discrepancy remains unresolved.

physicalDescription.extent: i-viii + 334 + ix-xiv folios, with losses and detached fragments
notes: The debate is written in 74 eight-line abab abab stanzas by Scribe 1.
The final five stanzas survive only as line beginnings on the mutilated f. 35ra stub.

rights.basis: The witness was produced in 1331-1340. This assessment concerns the medieval wording only, not a modern transcription, catalogue description, or photograph.
rights.component: Medieval textual content of the debate on ff. 31vb-35ra

access.accessMethod: Open the current Bodleian medieval manuscript record.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Laud Misc. 108
access.format: HTML and TEI XML catalogue description

dateStatements.label: Codex core and additions
dateStatements.notes: Hand B, responsible for ff. 200v-203v, is somewhat later than hands A and C but remains within the catalogue's late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century core description.
dateStatements.value: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century, with additions through the fourteenth century

entities.description: A composite English parchment codex; Hand B copied the debate at ff. 200v-203v, with a unique Old French prose transition and four-line benediction.

holding.notes: The debate begins partway down f. 200v after the preceding item and ends on f. 203v before the next item; both boundary surfaces are shared.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Laud Misc. 108

notes: The Hand B dialect is localized by the current catalogue to the Isle of Ely or possibly west Norfolk or north-east Suffolk.
The manuscript may have been produced at Oxford; the catalogue preserves this as a possibility rather than a certainty.

rights.basis: The relevant manuscript layer dates to the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century. This assessment concerns medieval wording only, not modern descriptions or photographs.
rights.component: Medieval textual content of the debate and its witness-specific French material on ff. 200v-203v

access.accessMethod: Open the current Bodleian medieval manuscript record.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)
access.format: HTML and TEI XML catalogue description

entities.description: A monumental Middle English religious and didactic anthology; Scribe 2 copied the debate at ff. 286rc-287rb.

holding.notes: The debate starts in the third column of f. 286r after The Prick of Love, fills f. 286v, and ends in the second column of f. 287r before the next item.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)
notes: The current catalogue and direct facsimile labels supersede Linow's legacy locator 'Bl. 285 v.' for present-day discovery, without silently erasing that historical foliation statement.

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i surviving leaves; originally viii + 413
notes: The manuscript is associated with the West Midlands; Scribe 2's dialect is localized more specifically to Worcestershire (Linguistic Profile 7630).
Seventy-one original leaves are lost.

rights.basis: The witness dates to c. 1390-1400. This assessment concerns medieval wording only, not modern descriptions or photographs.
rights.component: Medieval textual content of the debate on ff. 286rc-287rb

access.accessMethod: Open the current Bodleian medieval manuscript record.
access.format: HTML and TEI XML catalogue description

access.accessMethod: No complete public facsimile or public target-folio images were found through the exact catalog, archives record, and Digital Bodleian object search.
notes: The one public f. 11v image does not overlap ff. 136r-139v. This is a dated negative finding, not proof that no private surrogate exists.

access.accessMethod: Use Bodleian mediated copying to request either reference scans of ff. 136r-139v or a quote for the whole codex. For a whole-object facsimile, explicitly request covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and every leaf.
access.format: Made-to-order PDF reference scans, subject to condition and service assessment
notes: The current service page lists £1 per scan for non-Oxford users, a £10 minimum order, case-by-case assessment, and an approximate ten-working-day turnaround; obtain a current quote before relying on those figures.
Ordering scans and obtaining permission for redistribution or publication are separate questions. Record the quote, delivered-file notice, and written reuse terms if an order is fulfilled.

rights.basis: The service and Digital Bodleian terms distinguish access or delivery from permission for publication and redistribution. Exact rights cannot be known until the quote, order conditions, delivered-file notice, and any written permission are obtained.
rights.component: Any reference scans newly supplied by Bodleian mediated copying
notes: Do not infer image-level public-domain or repository-publication rights from the age of the manuscript wording.
Reassess this record against the terms actually supplied with any future order.

access.accessMethod: Register for a Bodleian Reader card, request the manuscript in advance, and consult current special-collections handling rules before travel.

entities.description: A London parchment codex dated 1425-1450; the debate is a later addition in different ink at ff. 136r-139v and is written continuously as prose in the manuscript.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Digby 102
notes: Linow's introductory description says f. 136v, but his text heading, the XML, DIMEV, and the current catalogue all give f. 136r; the current locus is used.
The work is a later addition with different ink and a separate decoration scheme.
DIMEV records that the verse is written as prose; Linow restores lineation in print.

rights.basis: The witness dates to 1425-1450. This assessment concerns medieval wording only, not modern descriptions, a supplied scan, or a photograph.
rights.component: Medieval textual content of the debate on ff. 136r-139v

rights.basis: The service and Digital Bodleian terms distinguish access or delivery from permission for publication and redistribution. Exact rights cannot be known until the quote, order conditions, delivered-file notice, and any written permission are obtained.

next_steps: Search Linow and Varnhagen archival papers, publisher records, and relevant German and Oxford library catalogues for working transcripts or correspondence.
If a transcript is located, record its physical identifier and compare its corrections and italic conventions with Linow's print.
notes: The paths are retained through the named transcripts because Linow explicitly identifies them as his immediate copy sources; survival and present location are not known.
openQuestions.question: Do the four nineteenth-century working transcripts used by Linow survive, and if so where?

next_steps: Check Linow's correspondence, Bodleian reader and copying records, Oxford directories, and Varnhagen papers for a full name.
Do not merge this person with a similarly named scholar without documentary support.
openQuestions.question: Who was the Oxford 'Mr. Parker' whom Linow credits with the Laud and Vernon transcripts?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian mediated copying for reference scans of ff. 136r-139v and separately for a quote covering the complete physical object.
For a whole-object quote, explicitly request covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and every leaf.
Record the delivered-file rights notice and request written clarification before redistributing or publishing supplied images.
notes: The cached one-canvas f. 11v object has zero target-work overlap and must not be described as resolving this question.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete or target-folio facsimile of MS. Digby 102 be obtained?

next_steps: Compare Linow's complete manuscript description and any nineteenth-century Bodleian foliation with current flyleaf and folio numbering.
Retain both the legacy and current locators until the crosswalk is documented.
openQuestions.question: What historical foliation system explains Linow's 'Bl. 285 v.' heading for a work now catalogued at ff. 286rc-287rb?

next_steps: Compare every XML HI REND='italic' span against the corresponding printed page and manuscript image.
Classify expansions, supplied letters, damaged readings, or other editorial uses individually rather than assigning one global meaning.
Obtain Digby target-folio images before attempting a complete four-witness assessment.
notes: No global editorial statement defining every italic occurrence was found.
openQuestions.question: Which exact manuscript features are represented by each italic span inherited from Linow in CME00130?

next_steps: Seek an explicit repository statement identifying the licensed components.
Until clarified, do not treat the software-focused root license as an unambiguous open-data license for CME00130.xml.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME submodule's Unlicense apply to XML textual data and markup, or only to software?

relations.subjectLabel: Digital Index of Middle English Verse record 605

reviewNotes: The primary transmission paths do not collapse Linow's named working transcripts: repository artifact -> U-M encoding -> Linow edition -> working transcript -> manuscript witness.

The four XML DIV2 streams are parallel witness representations serialized from an alternating-page print layout, not four abstract works. Raw XML counts include 34 empty one-line alignment groups; the descriptive manifest records nonempty verse metrics separately.

The complete cached manuscript bundles retain every image in the current public IIIF objects. Work-level locators point into those complete objects and preserve shared boundary surfaces rather than cropping them.

The Digby IIIF object is deliberately modeled as a selected-folio facsimile with partial coverage and zero target-work overlap. It is not evidence that the poem or the complete codex has been digitized.
DIMEV's French classification is supported only at the Laud-part level by that witness's unique Old French transition and four-line benediction; it is not generalized to the other versions.

Linow's legacy Vernon heading 'Bl. 285 v.' conflicts with the current Bodleian catalogue and direct canvas labels, which place the poem at ff. 286rc-287rb. The current locator is used and the conflict remains explicit.

Ordering scans and obtaining permission for redistribution or publication are separate questions. Record the quote, delivered-file notice, and written reuse terms if an order is fulfilled.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a version of the University of Michigan CME00130 electronic text, which encodes Wilhelm Linow's 1889 parallel edition of four Middle English manuscript versions on printed pp. 24-105. Linow says that his edition was prepared from four intermediate working transcripts: Hermann Varnhagen supplied those of the Auchinleck and Digby versions, while an incompletely identified Mr. Parker of Oxford supplied Laud and Vernon. Complete public IIIF facsimiles of the Auchinleck, Laud, and Vernon codices and a complete scan of Linow's book are cached with exact work locators. Digital Bodleian exposes only one selected Digby 102 image, f. 11v, which has no overlap with the poem at ff. 136r-139v; no complete public Digby facsimile was found, but made-to-order reference scans can be requested.

catalogSummary.title: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule

catalogingSubject.label: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English body-and-soul debate poem represented in CME00130 by four separately labeled witness streams: Auchinleck, Laud Misc. 108, Vernon, and Digby 102. The four DIV2 elements are serialized manuscript versions from Linow's parallel edition, not four independent works or intrinsic chapters of one text.

contentParts.label: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule

locators.value: BODY/DIV1 containing four witness-labeled DIV2 streams

notes: The child parts are witness representations for scoping metadata and lineage; they contribute zero additional abstract works to the work-level item count.
contentParts.summary: The anonymous body-and-soul debate as an abstract work, represented below by four parallel manuscript-version parts whose extents and source loci differ.

contentParts.label: Auchinleck manuscript version

locators.value: DIV2[1]; 76 LG and 594 L elements, including two one-line blank alignment placeholders

notes: The 592 figure counts nonempty XML L elements and agrees with DIMEV v1.0; the NLS headnote counts 591 lines while excluding Latin speech labels and the English title. The one-line discrepancy remains unresolved.
This is a witness representation used for part-scoped description and lineage, not an intrinsic division of the abstract poem.
contentParts.summary: Linow's reordered and lightly emended printing of the 74-stanza Auchinleck version. The surviving manuscript ends on a mutilated f. 35ra stub, leaving only the beginnings of lines in the final five stanzas.

contentParts.label: Laud Misc. 108 version

locators.value: DIV2[2]; 80 LG and 510 L elements, including eighteen one-line blank alignment placeholders; one prose P between LG 73 and 74

notes: The metrics count 61 eight-line Middle English stanzas plus one four-line Old French verse group; the intervening French prose sentence is not counted as a verse line.
This is a witness representation used for part-scoped description and lineage, not an intrinsic division of the abstract poem.
contentParts.summary: Linow's reordered and lightly emended printing of the 61-stanza Laud version, followed by its unique Old French prose transition and four-line scribal benediction.

contentParts.label: Vernon manuscript version

locators.value: DIV2[3]; 79 LG and 513 L elements, including seventeen one-line blank alignment placeholders

notes: Linow's printed heading and the XML preserve the legacy locator 'Bl. 285 v.'; the current Bodleian component record and direct images place the work at ff. 286rc-287rb.
This is a witness representation used for part-scoped description and lineage, not an intrinsic division of the abstract poem.
contentParts.summary: Linow's reordered and lightly emended printing of the 62-stanza Vernon version, beginning in the third column of f. 286r and ending in the second column of f. 287r.

contentParts.label: Digby 102 version

locators.value: DIV2[4]; 76 LG and 545 L elements, including nine one-line blank alignment placeholders
ff. 136r-139v; no complete public facsimile found

notes: Linow's descriptive paragraph says f. 136v, while his printed text heading, the XML heading, DIMEV, and the current Bodleian catalog give f. 136r. The current locus is used.
This is a witness representation used for part-scoped description and lineage, not an intrinsic division of the abstract poem.
contentParts.summary: Linow's reordered and lightly emended printing of the 67-stanza Digby version. The manuscript presents the poem as prose, while Linow and the XML restore verse lineation.

assertion.notes: The Laud witness establishes circulation by this outer bound; no authorial copy or exact composition date survives.

dateStatements.display: By the early fourteenth century at the latest

assertion.notes: The NLS headnote gives c. 1275-1325. The current Bodleian description dates the codex's core to the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century, gives 1290-1400 as the broader machine-readable range, and says the work's Hand B is somewhat later than Hands A and C. This is a witness date, not a composition date.

dateStatements.display: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century (Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108)

extent.description: One poem in four parallel witness versions. The XML has one DIV1, four DIV2 streams, 311 LG, 2,162 L, 82 PB, and 840 MILESTONE elements. Of the 2,162 L elements, 2,128 are nonempty textual lines and 34 are one-line blank alignment placeholders. Version extents are recorded separately because summing parallel copies would misstate the length of the abstract work: Auchinleck 74 stanzas / 592 encoded nonempty lines; Laud 61 eight-line Middle English stanzas plus a four-line Old French benediction / 492 verse lines; Vernon 62 stanzas / 496 lines; Digby 67 stanzas / 536 lines.

formStatements.description: A stanzaic dialogue poem, normally in eight-line abab abab stanzas. Linow and CME lineate all four streams as verse; DIMEV reports that Digby 102 writes its version as prose in the manuscript.

assertion.notes: A nocturnal vision frames the body-and-soul dialogue.
Short Latin rubrics and speech labels are embedded paratext rather than a second whole-work language.
DIMEV v1.0 exposes French at record level because of this Laud-only material. The other encoded witness versions and the abstract poem are not thereby made French or generally macaronic.

scope.description: One prose transition after the Laud witness's stanza 59 and a unique four-line scribal benediction

notes: The 840 MILESTONE elements are interleaved editorial and manuscript line-number systems, often duplicated at the same point; they are not additional verse lines.

Blank one-line LG elements preserve the vertical relationship of Linow's facing witness texts after the U-M encoding serialized the page streams. They are alignment machinery, not missing or empty stanzas in the abstract poem.
Current repository component records control manuscript loci. Linow's historical folio labels are preserved in part notes and lineage rather than silently normalized.

next_steps: Review recent work on the seven-witness tradition and its dialectal reconstruction.
Keep copyist dialects and codex production places scoped to individual witness versions unless scholarship establishes authorial origin.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be localized or dated more narrowly than circulation by the early fourteenth century in England?

next_steps: Compare line-by-line counting conventions in the NLS transcription, Linow pp. 24-64, and the damaged final five stanzas.
Preserve both attributed counts until the exact divergent line is identified.
openQuestions.question: Why does the NLS headnote count 591 Auchinleck lines while DIMEV v1.0 and the nonempty CME line elements count 592?

assertion.notes: This broad cultural origin does not localize composition more narrowly.
The NLS project describes the manuscript as produced in London in the 1330s. This does not establish the poem's original place of composition.

scope.description: Production context of the Auchinleck manuscript copy

assertion.notes: The current catalog localizes Hand B to the Isle of Ely, or possibly west Norfolk or north-east Suffolk. The uncertainty is preserved at the broader East Anglian level.

scope.description: Scribal dialect of Laud Hand B

assertion.notes: The current catalog assigns Scribe 2 to Worcestershire, Linguistic Atlas profile LP 7630. This is a copyist's dialect, not a composition place.
The current catalog gives London as the codex's origin. The poem is a later addition within that codex, so the statement is scoped to the surviving copy rather than the abstract work.
No named Middle English author is supported. Attributions associated with other Latin or vernacular body-and-soul debates are not transferred to this poem.

scope.description: The four witness texts on printed pp. 24-105
Reconstructed stanza order, cross-witness emendation, notes, and Simeon variants in the 1889 edition
German introduction and notes on printed pp. 1-23 and 107-112, outside CME00130's encoded text
Working transcript used by Linow for the Auchinleck text
Working transcript used by Linow for the Digby text

assertion.notes: The role is explicit; the transcriber's fuller identity remains unresolved.

scope.description: Working transcript used by Linow for the Laud text

assertion.notes: The role is explicit; the transcriber's fuller identity remains unresolved.

scope.description: Working transcript used by Linow for the Vernon text

summaries.value: In a night vision, a soul confronts its dead body. The two blame one another for their shared sins, recall worldly vanity and bodily decay, and face demonic punishment; the waking narrator closes with an appeal to repentance and divine mercy.

CME00130 serializes the four witness texts that Linow printed on alternating pages: Auchinleck and Laud first, then Vernon and Digby. It excludes Linow's introduction, Simeon variants, textual notes, Old French appendix, and Theodore Martin adaptation.

DIMEV v1.0 groups all seven known witnesses under record 605 / IMEV and NIMEV 351. Its record-level French flag derives from Laud's unique French sentence and benediction; French is therefore recorded only for the Laud version here.

assertion.notes: A cataloging title based on the Auchinleck rubric and Linow's title; it is not asserted as authorial.

titles.value: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
Þe desputisoun bitven þe bodi and þe soule
A disputacion bytwene þe bodi and þe soule

assertion.notes: Each witness has its own spelling and, in places, wording; this normalized form is an index incipit, not a diplomatic transcription.

scope.description: DIMEV's normalized opening across the witness versions

titles.value: As I lay in a winters night / In a darkening before the day
Explicit disputacio inter corpus et animam
